data.title: York Space Systems rises on Q4 beat; Needham cuts target to $33
data.description:
York Space Systems (YSS) shares jumped today over 23% to ~$21.86 as trading began after the company reported Q4 results that beat estimates, helping the stock recover from recent losses but remain down ~6% W/W from ~$20.20 and ~17% M/M from ~$25.91.

Following the report, Needham & Company lowered its price target by 9%, from $42 to $33, while maintaining a buy rating.

Q4 revenue rose 37.5% Y/Y to ~$105.35M, coming in higher than estimates by $2.23M, while gross profit surged 133% Y/Y to ~$75.5M, showing strong profitability. 

Net loss reduced by 15% to ~$84.5M, and adjusted EBITDA loss improved significantly to ~$8.3M from ~$43.0M last year. This momentum reflects wide execution across the year. 

FY25 revenue rose 52% to ~$386M, mainly because of the firm’s strong execution of its transport layer contracts, which also turned ~$319M of backlog into actual revenue and finished the year with a ~$543M backlog.

CEO Dirk Wallinger said, “2025 was the year York defined what a modern mission prime looks like,” highlighting progress with the Department of Defense and satellite deployments.

The company’s January IPO acted as a strong support to its financial growth, with total liquidity rising to ~$895.4M. Looking ahead, FY26 revenue is expected to be around $545M–$595M, out of which +70% is already secured from backlog, giving the company a strong portfolio and visibility in the financial markets.

data.title: Cardinal Infrastructure Group to be added to Russell 2000, Russell 3000 indexes
data.description:
• Cardinal Infrastructure Group (CDNL) will be added to the Russell 2000 and Russell 3000 indexes, as part of FTSE Russell's first quarter Initial Public Offering (IPO) addition, effective March 23, 2026, the company said on Friday.
  • Russell indexes are widely used by investment managers and institutional investors for index funds and as benchmarks for active investment strategies.

data.title: Brent could surpass $130/bbl if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed – analyst
data.description:
Brent crude (CO1:COM) could surge past $130 per barrel if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed for another two to four weeks, according to Matt Smith, Kpler’s lead oil analyst.

Smith warned in an interview with CNBC that markets are not fully accounting for the severity of the ongoing supply disruption, noting there is “complacency in the oil markets right now.”

The strait, a critical chokepoint for global energy supplies, remains effectively shut down.

“For all intents and purposes, it is closed,” Smith said, explaining that Iran is acting as a “gatekeeper,” allowing only its own crude cargoes and a handful of other vessels to pass through.

Approximately 15M barrels of daily crude exports and 5M barrels of refined products that previously transited the waterway are no longer moving.

The blockage is creating cascading effects across Asian markets. Refineries that depend on Middle Eastern crude are scaling back operations because shipments are not arriving, leading to what Smith described as “demand destruction.”

Shortages in diesel and jet fuel are intensifying faster than crude price increases, threatening mobility across the region in the weeks ahead.

Recent escalations, including Israel’s strike on the South Pars gas field and Iran’s retaliatory drone attacks on energy infrastructure, have heightened tensions without moving toward resolution.

Smith characterized the exchange as “tit for tat,” that is “upping the ante,” while noting that targeting gas rather than oil infrastructure was likely intentional.

“The last thing that President Trump wants to be doing right now is targeting oil infrastructure because that’s only going to rally the price of oil,” he said.

The sustained price surge is creating significant obstacles for the global economy. Smith warned that rising commodity costs will likely fuel inflation as input prices climb.

“It definitely provides big headwinds to the global economy for sure because every single region is being affected by this,” he said, though he stopped short of predicting a recession.

The U.S. administration has deployed multiple strategies to contain prices, including SPR releases, ethanol waivers, and discussions about easing sanctions on Iranian crude.

Smith noted that removing 1.7M barrels per day of Iranian exports from the market would only push prices higher, explaining why some Iranian cargoes continue reaching China despite the broader blockade.

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL).

data.title: Academy Sports & Outdoors offering branded Mastercard
data.description:
Academy Sports and Outdoors (ASO) is offering a co-branded credit with Mastercard (MA) and in partnership with Bread Financial (BFH), offering members rewards for purchases, exclusive offers, free shipping on Academy online purchases, and rewards up to $25 for every $500 spent at Academy during a calendar year.

The credit card is managed through Synchrony Bank. 

The myAcademy Rewards Mastercard Credit Card, along with a “reimagined” myAcademy Rewards program, was designed to increase purchases at the sporting goods store while also offering cardholders cash back at non-Academy stores. Cardholders earn 5% off every day at Academy (ASO) and an unlimited 2% in rewards on everyday purchases.

Most interest rates for Synchrony Mastercards are within a range of 17% to as high as 33%, depending on the cardholders’ credit score.

data.title: Fed Gov. Waller: From supporting an immediate rate cut to ‘wait and see’ stance
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller has shifted from supporting an immediate rate cut to a cautious “wait and see” stance following the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East.

In an interview with CNBC, Waller explained that what initially appeared to be a short-lived spike in oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM) now looks like a more protracted conflict.

“Caution is warranted… I just want to wait and see where this goes,” he said, adding that he remains open to advocating for rate cuts later this year if conditions improve.

Waller drew a sharp distinction between temporary oil price spikes and sustained increases that threaten to spread into core inflation. Because oil serves as a fundamental input across the economy, prolonged high prices carry different risks than isolated tariff shocks on specific goods.

“Oil is a major intermediate import, and it will at some point bleed through,” Waller warned, noting this concern factored heavily into his decision to support holding rates steady.

The Fed governor offered a mathematical argument for why he believes underlying inflation pressures are actually easing despite headline numbers remaining elevated.

With inflation holding at roughly 2.8% since December 2024—even as tariffs added an estimated 50 to 100 basis points of price pressure—Waller reasoned that structural inflation must be declining.

“If tariffs had any pass-through effect, then structural had to come down to maintain 2.8,” he explained, suggesting the true underlying rate is getting closer to the Fed’s 2% target.

On the labor market, Waller acknowledged grappling with a new economic reality where zero net job growth may be sufficient to maintain stable unemployment, given near-zero labor force growth projections.

Despite recent negative job reports, including a 92,000 job decline, he noted the math suggests the labor market remains in equilibrium.

“If things go reasonably well, and the labor market continues to be weak, I would start advocating again for cutting the policy rate later this year,” Waller said.

The governor also weighed in on proposals to shrink the Fed’s balance sheet and expressed concerns about emerging risks in private credit markets. He criticized any plan to return to a “scarce reserves” regime without corresponding reductions in reserve demand.

“Reducing the balance sheet, holding reserve demand constant, and moving to scarce, to me, that’s just idiotic,” Waller stated. He also flagged growing unease about private credit funds that allow redemptions like checking accounts, warning that such structures introduce run risk.

Looking ahead, Waller acknowledged the Fed faces difficult trade-offs between addressing inflation and preventing economic weakening. He noted that unemployment historically rises quickly through “herd behavior” among corporations, and sustained oil price shocks could tip consumer confidence and spending.

While he stopped short of predicting a recession, Waller made clear the Fed stands ready to respond if labor markets deteriorate significantly—even with inflation running above target.

More on the U.S. economy

  • Jeffrey Gundlach warns Fed rate hike may be on horizon
  • Is U.S. manufacturing 'roaring back' under Trump? The dual data reality
  • Fed's Bowman pencils in three rate cuts for 2026 as labor market concerns linger
  • Household net worth hits new high of $184T in Q4, aided by stock rally

data.title: Silgan interested in Gerresheimer takeover - report
data.description:
Silgan Holdings (SLGN) has indicated interest in acquiring German packaging maker Gerresheimer (GRRMF), according to a  Reuters report on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter. 

Silgan is working with advisors on a potential offer that could value Gerresheimer (GRRMF) at 41 euros per share, one person told Reuters.

Gerresheimer declined to comment to Reuters. Silgan was ​not immediately available for comment.
 
Bloomberg reported last March that a consortium of private equity firms, including Warburg Pincus and KKR & Co. (KKR), were in advanced discussions to buy Gerresheimer (GRRMF). 
 
Gerresheimer jumped 23% in Germany on the Reuters news. Silgan fell 1.4% on Friday.

data.title: Apple's App Store growth slows slightly while iPhone demand jumps: Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Apple's (AAPL) App Store revenue growth has declined slightly this month, but a year-over-year increase in iPhone builds demonstrates strong demand, according to data from Morgan Stanley and Sensor Tower.

"Based on the latest disclosures from Sensor Tower, we estimate App Store net revenue is growing 6% Y/Y month-to-date through March 18th, a 310 bps deceleration from February off a 170 bps tougher Y/Y compare," said Morgan Stanley analysts, led by Erik Woodring, in a Friday investor report. "This implies that QTD through March 18th, App Store net revenue is growing 7.0% Y/Y, tracking 1 point behind our +8.0% Y/Y March quarter App Store forecast."

On a regional basis, the App Store's revenue is up 4% year over year in China, while the U.S. and Japan markets were down 2% and 5%, respectively.

However, iPhone builds for the quarter reached 52M units, which demonstrates a 12% year-over-year increase and points to higher shipment volumes ahead.

"Based on the historical builds-to-shipments relationship, 52M June quarter builds would imply 57-58M iPhone shipments — largely in-line with our 58M unit forecast, but 7M units above Consensus shipments at 50M — reinforcing our above-Street June quarter revenue forecasts," Woodring said. "We see this iPhone build strength as evidence of sustained iPhone end demand amidst 3nm wafer constraints, consistent with our view that Apple's additional wafer orders placed late last year should support upside to shipments in both C1Q26 and C2Q26."

data.title: Corcept rises after insider buy worth over $3M
data.description:
• Corcept Therapeutics (CORT) traded higher on Friday after the company’s board member Leonard Baker Jr. disclosed that he purchased shares of the Korlym maker in a transaction worth more than $3M earlier this week.
  • According to a regulatory filing on Thursday, Baker, Jr., who has been serving on the company's board as a director since 1999, has purchased 100,000 shares of Corcept (CORT) for an average price of about $33.14 apiece.
  • The share purchases worth more than $3.3M have increased Baker Jr.'s direct ownership in the company by ~2% to ~1.1M shares.
  • The purchases took place in two separate transactions on Tuesday, less than a month after an appeals court issued an unfavorable ruling against Corcept (CORT) in its patent dispute with Teva Pharmaceuticals (TEVA) related to Korlym.

data.title: Sector breakdown since the start of the U.S.–Iran conflict: Energy leads, materials lags
data.description:
Market volatility has jumped since the outbreak of the Iran–U.S. conflict on February 28, prompting investors to reassess risk exposure and rotate toward defensive and opportunity-rich sectors. Performance across the 11 S&P 500 sectors has diverged notably, reflecting shifting sentiment amid geopolitical uncertainty.

Energy has emerged as the clear outperformer, benefiting from heightened concerns over supply disruptions and rising commodity prices. In contrast, materials have lagged, facing pressure as investors pull back from the precious metals and mining space. 

The broader market response underscores a search for clarity as participants evaluate which sectors offer resilience and upside potential, and which remain most vulnerable as tensions in the Middle East continue to evolve. 

Outlined below is how all 11 sectors have performed since the outbreak in the Middle East:

  1. State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) +6.9%
  2. State Street Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK), -0.9%.
  3. State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) -2.4%.
  4. State Street Communication Services Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLC), -4.4%. 
  5. State Street Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE) -4.5%
  6. State Street Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) -4.7%. 
  7. State Street Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLY) -6.8%.
  8. State Street Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLI), -7.6%. 
  9. State Street Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV) -8.5%. 
  10. State Street Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP) -8.7% 
  11. State Street Materials Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLB) -10.5%

Communication Services ETFs: (XLC), (VOX), (IYZ), (RSPC), and (XTL).

Consumer Discretionary ETFs: (XLY), (VCR), (FXD), (FDIS), (RSPD), and (RXI).

Consumer Staples ETFs: (XLP), (VDC), (IYK), (FSTA), (KXI), and (RSPS).

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), (AMLP), and (IXC).

Financials ETFs: (XLF), (VFH), (IYF), (FNCL), (IYG), and (FXO).

Health Care ETFs: (XLV), (VHT), (IHI), (IXJ), (IYH), (FHLC), and (FXH).

Industrials ETFs: (XLI), (VIS), (FXR), (IYJ), (FIDU), and (RSPN).

Materials ETFs: (XLB), (VAW), (IYM), (FXZ), (MXI), and (RSPM).

Real Estate ETFs: (VNQ), (XLRE), (IYR), (USRT), and (HOMZ).

Tech ETFs: (VGT), (XLK), (IYW), (FTEC), (IXN), and (RSPT).

Utilities ETFs: (XLU), (VPU), (FUTY), (IDU), (FXU), and (JXI).

data.title: Super Micro crashes after Chinese smuggling allegations; analyst calls it a 'train wreck'
data.description:
Super Micro Computer (SMCI) shares crashed more than 28% on Friday after three individuals linked to the company, including a co-founder, were charged with assisting in the smuggling of at least $2.5B worth of AI technology to China. 

Seeking Alpha analyst Jeremy of Kumquat Research called the AI server maker a “train wreck.”

“Super Micro's governance issues have been a train wreck in slow motion,” Jeremy said in an email. “Compliance and governance issues have plagued the company for years, whether it's delayed regulatory filings or the departure of EY due to supposed accounting irregularities. The current indictment could be the nail in the coffin, as Nvidia and other chipmakers will likely have to divert shipments from Super Micro due to this issue and due to the company's increasingly risky balance sheet position.”

Conversely, shares of Dell Technologies (DELL), which competes with Super Micro in the AI server market, rose on Friday, bucking a broader market sell-off.

U.S. prosecutors did not name Super ‌Micro in the complaint, referring only to a “U.S. manufacturer.” San Jose, California-based Super Micro said it was informed by federal prosecutors of the indictment on Thursday. It noted that the company itself was not named as a defendant in the case and said it had cooperated with investigators.

In an indictment unsealed on Thursday, the U.S. government alleged that Yih-Shyan “Wally” Liaw, Ruei-Tsan, “Steven” Chang, and Ting-Wei “Willy” Sun worked together to violate the Export Control Reform Act. Liaw co-founded Super ​Micro in 1993, and joined its board of directors in 2023. Chang was a sales manager in the Taiwan office of Super Micro, while Sun was ⁠a contractor.

U.S. officials allege the trio went to great lengths to hide their actions from both U.S.-based server manufacturers and export control authorities. They allegedly even used hair dryers to remove labels and serial numbers ​from the real machines and placed them on dummy machines left behind after the real machines had been shipped to China.

The efforts had yielded around $2.5B in sales for the server maker since 2024, with $510M sold between late April 2025 and mid-May 2025 going to the Southeast Asian company and on to China, the indictment said. The plaintiff said the server maker had no U.S. Commerce Department license to export servers featuring Nvidia's (NVDA) GPUs to China.

Super Micro said it placed Liaw and Chang on leave and terminated its ties with Sun, who ​was a contractor, after being made aware of the charges on Thursday.

(Seeking Alpha's Arundhati Sarkar contributed to this story.)
